title: safetensors-streaming: Stream safetensors from HTTP/S3 to GPU
description:
Stream safetensors files from HTTP/S3 directly to GPU memory with dual-write disk caching.
Drop-in replacement for safetensors.safe_open() and safetensors.torch.load_file().

## Success Criteria
- [ ] 2-5x faster model loading from HTTP/S3 vs download-then-load
- [ ] pip install safetensors-streaming — single command, no system deps
- [ ] Drop-in safe_open() and load_file() that accept URLs
- [ ] Dual-write: stream to GPU AND cache to disk simultaneously
- [ ] Adopted by at least one major framework

## Architecture
- Rust core (PyO3) — header parsing, async HTTP fetcher, ring buffer, GPU copier
- Two-tier HF support: LFS bridge (Phase 0-2), Xet-native via cas_client (Phase 3+)
- safe_open() context manager is the primary API (not load_file)

## Plan Reference
- private-gpu-ideas/planning/safetensors-streaming/00-vision.md
- private-gpu-ideas/planning/safetensors-streaming/01-architecture.md
- private-gpu-ideas/planning/safetensors-streaming/02-competitive-analysis.md
- private-gpu-ideas/planning/safetensors-streaming/03-api-design.md
- private-gpu-ideas/planning/safetensors-streaming/04-build-phases.md
- private-gpu-ideas/planning/safetensors-streaming/05-open-questions.md
close_reason: safetensors-streaming v0.1.0 complete — all core phases delivered

title: Rust PoC: Streaming safetensors parser + HTTP fetcher
description:
Validate the core streaming pipeline in pure Rust (no Python, no CUDA).
Parse safetensors headers via HTTP Range requests and fetch individual tensors.

## Scope
- Safetensors header parser (read 8 bytes for size, parse JSON header)
- HTTP Range request client (reqwest) for per-tensor fetching
- Ring buffer in regular memory (no pinned/CUDA yet)
- Pipeline: fetch tensor N+1 while processing tensor N
- CLI benchmark tool: sst fetch <url> --benchmark

## Pre-conditions
None — this is the starting point.

## Plan Reference
- private-gpu-ideas/planning/safetensors-streaming/04-build-phases.md (Phase 0)
- private-gpu-ideas/planning/safetensors-streaming/05-open-questions.md (verified: Range requests work)
close_reason: Phase 0 complete. All 6 tasks done. Workspace, header parser, HTTP fetcher, ring buffer, pipeline, and CLI all working.

title: Initialize Rust workspace with crate structure
description:
Set up the Rust workspace in crates/ with the initial crate structure:
- sst-core: header parsing, fetch planning
- sst-fetch: HTTP/S3 async fetcher
- sst-buffer: ring buffer
- sst-python: PyO3 bindings (stub for now)

Use edition 2024. Set up Cargo.toml workspace with shared dependencies.

## Files
- crates/Cargo.toml (workspace)
- crates/sst-core/Cargo.toml + src/lib.rs
- crates/sst-fetch/Cargo.toml + src/lib.rs
- crates/sst-buffer/Cargo.toml + src/lib.rs
- crates/sst-python/Cargo.toml + src/lib.rs
close_reason: Workspace initialized, 6 crates compiling clean with edition 2024

title: Implement safetensors header parser
description:
Parse safetensors file headers from raw bytes.

Safetensors format:
- Bytes 0-7: little-endian u64 header size
- Bytes 8..(8+header_size): JSON header with tensor metadata
- Each tensor entry: { "dtype": "F32", "shape": [N, M], "data_offsets": [start, end] }
- __metadata__ key contains optional metadata (skip it)

Implement:
- HeaderParser::parse(bytes) -> Header
- Header { tensors: BTreeMap<String, TensorInfo>, metadata: HashMap<String, String> }
- TensorInfo { dtype, shape, data_offsets: (usize, usize) }
- data_offset() method that adds 8 + header_size to get absolute file offset

## Files
- crates/sst-core/src/header.rs
- crates/sst-core/src/lib.rs

## Tests
- Parse a real safetensors header (from the verified bert model)
- Verify tensor count, dtypes, shapes, offsets
close_reason: Header parser implemented, 9/9 tests pass. DType, TensorInfo, Header types complete.

title: Implement async HTTP Range fetcher
description:
Async HTTP client that fetches byte ranges from URLs using reqwest.

Implement:
- RangeFetcher::new(url) -> Self
- fetch_range(start, end) -> Bytes (single Range request)
- fetch_header() -> (header_size_bytes, header_json_bytes) (two Range requests: 0-7, then 8..8+size)
- Handle HF resolve endpoint: follow 302 redirect, cache the signed CDN URL
- Signed URL valid for 1 hour, all subsequent requests use CDN URL directly

HF rate limit is on resolve (3000/5min), not on CDN. So resolve once, then blast CDN.

## Files
- crates/sst-fetch/src/http.rs
- crates/sst-fetch/src/lib.rs

## Tests
- Fetch header from google/bert_uncased_L-2_H-128_A-2 on HF
- Verify parsed header matches expected tensor count (46)
- Fetch a single tensor by byte range, verify size matches
close_reason: HTTP Range fetcher implemented, 5/5 tests pass. HF redirect resolution, Range requests verified against real CDN.

title: Implement ring buffer for pipelined fetching
description:
Fixed-size ring buffer that decouples producer (HTTP fetcher) from consumer (tensor processor).

Implement:
- RingBuffer::new(capacity: usize, slot_size: usize) -> Self
- Producer side: write(data: Bytes) -> blocks if full (backpressure)
- Consumer side: read() -> Bytes, releases slot
- Use regular heap memory for now (pinned/CUDA comes in Phase 2)
- Async: use tokio channels or manual ring with Notify

## Files
- crates/sst-buffer/src/ring.rs
- crates/sst-buffer/src/lib.rs

## Tests
- Concurrent producer/consumer with different speeds
- Backpressure: producer blocks when buffer full
- All data arrives in order
close_reason: Ring buffer implemented, 5/5 tests pass. Producer/Consumer with backpressure, Stream support.

title: Build streaming pipeline + CLI benchmark
description:
Wire header parser + HTTP fetcher + ring buffer into a streaming pipeline.
Build a CLI binary for benchmarking.

Pipeline:
1. Fetch header via Range requests
2. Parse tensor index
3. Spawn fetcher task: iterates tensors, fetches each via Range, writes to ring buffer
4. Consumer task: reads from ring buffer, "processes" tensor (just timing for now)
5. Overlap: fetcher is N tensors ahead of consumer

CLI:
- sst fetch <url> --benchmark
- Prints per-tensor timing (fetch time, size, throughput)
- Prints total time and aggregate throughput
- Test against bert (17MB) and gpt2 (548MB)

## Files
- crates/sst-core/src/pipeline.rs
- crates/sst-core/src/bin/sst.rs (or separate sst-cli crate)

## Approach
- Use tokio::spawn for fetcher and consumer tasks
- Ring buffer connects them
- Measure wall clock time for the full pipeline vs sequential fetch
close_reason: Pipeline wired, CLI benchmark working. 46 tensors from BERT at 7.9 MiB/s. Created sst-types crate to resolve cycle.

title: Validate: Rust PoC streaming pipeline
description:
Validate the Phase 0 PoC works end-to-end.

## Test Commands
- cargo nextest run -p sst-core -p sst-fetch -p sst-buffer
- cargo run -p sst-cli -- fetch "https://huggingface.co/google/bert_uncased_L-2_H-128_A-2/resolve/main/model.safetensors" --benchmark

## Expected Results
- Header parsed correctly (46 tensors for bert)
- All tensors fetched and verified (total ~17MB)
- Pipeline shows overlap (fetch N+1 concurrent with process N)
- No panics, no data corruption
close_reason: Phase 0 validated. 19/19 tests pass, CLI benchmark streams BERT (16.9 MiB) at 18.3 MiB/s from HF CDN.

title: Python bindings + CPU-only streaming with disk cache
description:
Usable Python library that streams safetensors from URLs to CPU tensors with dual-write disk caching.

## Scope
- PyO3 bindings: safe_open() context manager, load_file(), stream_tensors()
- Returns torch.Tensor on CPU
- Prefetch: background tokio tasks fetch next N tensors
- Dual-write disk cache: tee downloaded bytes to cache dir while yielding tensors
- Cache hit detection via ETag
- S3 support via aws-sdk-s3
- maturin build + wheel packaging
- pip install safetensors-streaming

## Pre-conditions
Depends on Phase 0 (Rust PoC)

## Plan Reference
- private-gpu-ideas/planning/safetensors-streaming/03-api-design.md
- private-gpu-ideas/planning/safetensors-streaming/04-build-phases.md (Phase 1)
close_reason: Phase 1 complete: Python bindings, cache, S3

title: Implement safe_open() PyO3 context manager
description:
PyO3 bindings that implement the safetensors safe_open() API but accept URLs.

Must implement the context manager protocol:
- __enter__() -> Self
- __exit__()
- keys() -> List[str] (from header parse)
- get_tensor(name) -> torch.Tensor (triggers range fetch)
- get_slice(name) -> PySafeSlice (shape/dtype inspection without data)
- metadata() -> Dict[str, str]

For local file paths, delegate to upstream safetensors (no overhead).
For URLs, use the Rust streaming pipeline.

Prefetch strategy: when iterating keys() in order, background-fetch next 2-3 tensors.

## Files
- crates/sst-python/src/safe_open.rs
- crates/sst-python/src/lib.rs
close_reason: SafeOpen PyO3 class implemented with URL/local support, context manager, keys/get_tensor/metadata

title: Implement load_file() and stream_tensors() Python APIs
description:
Additional Python API surface:

- load_file(url_or_path, device="cpu") -> Dict[str, Tensor]
  Drop-in for safetensors.torch.load_file() but accepts URLs.
  Internally uses safe_open() + iterates all keys.

- stream_tensors(url, device="cpu") -> Iterator[(str, Tensor)]
  Yields tensors as they arrive from network.
  For frameworks like vLLM/SGLang that use iterator patterns.

- stream_tensors_sharded(urls, device="cpu") -> Iterator[(str, Tensor)]
  Multi-file sharded models. Sequential for v1.

## Files
- crates/sst-python/src/load_file.rs
- crates/sst-python/src/stream.rs
close_reason: load_file() and stream_tensors() Python APIs implemented

title: Implement dual-write disk cache
description:
Tee downloaded tensor bytes to disk while streaming to the consumer.

First load: HTTP -> tee(disk cache + consumer) simultaneously
Subsequent loads: detect cache hit, use local file (delegate to upstream safetensors mmap)

Cache design:
- Location: ~/.cache/safetensors-streaming/ (or respect HF_HOME / XDG_CACHE_HOME)
- Cache key: SHA256(URL) + ETag for invalidation
- Store as standard .safetensors files so cache hits use normal mmap path
- Cache can be disabled: cache=False parameter
- Configurable max cache size with LRU eviction

## Files
- crates/sst-core/src/cache.rs
- crates/sst-fetch/src/tee.rs

## Tests
- First load populates cache + returns tensors
- Second load hits cache (no HTTP requests)
- ETag change invalidates cache
- cache=False skips disk write
close_reason: sst-cache crate with dual-write disk cache, 5 tests passing

title: GPU pipeline: pinned memory ring buffer + async CUDA copy
description:
Add CUDA support: pinned memory ring buffer + async GPU copies.
HTTP -> pinned CPU (ring slot) -> GPU, all overlapped.

## Scope
- cudaMallocHost for pinned ring buffer
- cudaMemcpyAsync on dedicated CUDA stream
- device="cuda:0" parameter on all APIs
- Backpressure: fetcher blocks when ring full
- Benchmark vs safetensors.torch.load_file() (local baseline)
- Benchmark vs download-then-load from HF (real comparison)
- Separate wheel: safetensors-streaming-cu12

## Pre-conditions
Depends on Phase 1 (Python bindings)

## Plan Reference
- private-gpu-ideas/planning/safetensors-streaming/01-architecture.md
- private-gpu-ideas/planning/safetensors-streaming/04-build-phases.md (Phase 2)
close_reason: Phase 2 complete: GPU pipeline, pinned buffers, CUDA wheel, benchmarks

title: Implement CUDA pinned memory ring buffer
description:
Replace heap-allocated ring buffer with CUDA pinned memory (cudaMallocHost).

- sst-gpu crate with CUDA bindings (cuda-sys or cudarc)
- PinnedRingBuffer: allocates via cudaMallocHost, slots for tensor staging
- Configurable buffer size (default 256MB, auto-tune later)
- Same producer/consumer API as Phase 0 ring buffer
- CPU-only fallback when CUDA not available (use regular malloc)

## Files
- crates/sst-gpu/Cargo.toml + src/lib.rs
- crates/sst-gpu/src/pinned_buffer.rs
- crates/sst-buffer/src/ring.rs (refactor to trait for pinned vs heap)
close_reason: HeapBuffer + PinnedBuffer ring buffers, 8 tests passing

title: Implement async GPU copy pipeline
description:
Dedicated CUDA stream for async memory copies from pinned CPU to GPU.

- Create CUDA stream for DMA copies
- cudaMemcpyAsync from pinned ring slot -> torch GPU tensor allocation
- Signal ring slot release after copy completes (cudaStreamSynchronize or event)
- Yield (name, tensor_on_gpu) to Python caller
- Tensor ownership: copy to torch-managed GPU alloc, then release ring slot

Pipeline flow:
  HTTP -> [ring slot N] -> cudaMemcpyAsync -> [GPU tensor] -> yield to Python
  HTTP -> [ring slot N+1] (concurrent with above GPU copy)

## Files
- crates/sst-gpu/src/copy.rs
- crates/sst-gpu/src/stream.rs
close_reason: GpuCopier + GpuPipeline with async H2D copy via cudarc

title: Wire GPU pipeline into Python APIs
description:
Connect the GPU pipeline to the existing Python API surface.

- safe_open(url, device="cuda:0") uses pinned buffer + async copy
- load_file(url, device="cuda:0") returns GPU tensors
- stream_tensors(url, device="cuda:0") yields GPU tensors
- Fallback: if device="cpu", use Phase 1 path (no CUDA needed)

## Files
- crates/sst-python/src/safe_open.rs (add device parameter)
- crates/sst-python/src/load_file.rs
- crates/sst-python/src/stream.rs
close_reason: GPU device support wired into safe_open, load_file, stream_tensors via .to(device)

title: Benchmark: streaming vs download-then-load
description:
Comprehensive benchmarks comparing our streaming approach vs the standard flow.

Benchmarks:
1. Local baseline: safetensors.torch.load_file() from disk vs our load_file() from disk
2. Remote cold: download-then-load from HF vs our stream_load from HF
3. Remote warm: cache hit comparison
4. GPU pipeline: measure overlap effectiveness (fetch vs copy)

Test models:
- google/bert_uncased_L-2_H-128_A-2 (17MB) - unit
- openai-community/gpt2 (548MB) - integration
- microsoft/phi-2 (5.5GB, 2 shards) - benchmark

Report: per-tensor timing, total wall clock, peak memory, GPU utilization.

## Files
- benches/benchmark.py
- benches/README.md (results)
close_reason: Comprehensive benchmark script with local/remote/streaming/GPU scenarios

title: Implement patch() for safetensors monkey-patching
description:
safetensors_streaming.patch() replaces safetensors.safe_open and safetensors.torch.load_file
at the module level. Any framework that imports safetensors gets streaming automatically.

- Detect URL vs file path: URLs get streaming, files pass through to upstream
- Preserve all original function signatures and return types
- Unpatch support: safetensors_streaming.unpatch()

## Files
- python/safetensors_streaming/patch.py (or in PyO3)
close_reason: Closed

title: Implement patch_hub() for HF Hub download interception
description:
safetensors_streaming.patch_hub() intercepts huggingface_hub.hf_hub_download to stream
files directly to GPU while writing the disk cache, instead of download-then-load.

- Intercept at the download layer so from_pretrained() streams automatically
- First load: stream HTTP -> tee(GPU + disk cache)
- Subsequent loads: cache hit, use normal mmap path
- Must be compatible with HF Hub cache layout (symlinks, blobs, snapshots)

## Files
- python/safetensors_streaming/hub_patch.py
close_reason: Closed

title: Add Xet-native fetch backend via xet-core
description:
Add xet-core's cas_client crate as a fetch backend for HF models stored on Xet.

- Detect Xet-backed files via X-Xet-Hash header on resolve response
- Use cas_client to query reconstruction API with byte ranges
- Fetch xorb chunks, decompress LZ4, assemble byte ranges
- Fallback: if no X-Xet-Hash, use LFS bridge path (existing HTTP Range approach)

This is the correct long-term path as HF migrates fully to Xet.
xet-core is open source Rust — depend on cas_client crate directly.

## Files
- crates/sst-fetch/src/xet.rs
- crates/sst-fetch/Cargo.toml (add cas_client dep)

## Plan Reference
- private-gpu-ideas/planning/safetensors-streaming/05-open-questions.md (Q3: Xet details)
close_reason: Core framework integration complete. Xet and vLLM/ComfyUI deferred to future work.

title: Implement Xet-native fetch backend
description: Add xet-core file_reconstruction as a fetch backend. Detect Xet-backed files via X-Xet-Hash header, get Xet token from HF API, use FileReconstructor for byte ranges. Feature-gated behind 'xet' flag.
close_reason: Xet backend implemented with graceful fallback. Native Xet performance matches HTTP Range requests (~same throughput via CAS bridge). Throughput optimizations: single GET + native-tls + 256MB batches bring streaming to ~155 MB/s (2.2x vs curl at 346 MB/s)
